segments.text: And so to our first person tonight, Michelle Bachelet.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: She served not just once, but twice as the President of Chile.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: She was the first woman to hold that office.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And she went on to head...
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Yes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Michelle Bachelet, she likes doing everything in twos.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Twice President Bachelet, she went on to head two UN agencies, UN Women, UN Commissioner for Human Rights.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: She trained as a pediatrician, but ended up being the Minister of Defense.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: She is nominated by Brazil and also by Mexico.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Welcome.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Michelle Bachelet, let us begin.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: We're going to allow you five minutes to set out your vision for why you want this job and what are you going to do with it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And I have to tell you that discipline is a part of democracy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I'll give you a one minute warning when four minutes are up and then I'll have to say it ends there at five minutes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: The floor is yours.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Please do this because I'm Latin American, so do that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, the excellencies and dear friends, my candidacy for Secretary General of the United Nations is grounded on one clear conviction.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We must build the United Nations the world needs.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: A UN that not merely administered the present, but one with the capacity to transform it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: An organization that's more effective
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: more transparent, closer to the people, and capable of delivering concrete results.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: My own vision was born of a difficult and sometimes improbable miracle of dialogue, even though when all odds were against it, I have seen it transform the impossible into the achievable.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Trust in the international order and trust in our institutions are mutually reinforcing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: both require robust experience and firm commitment to the short term.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I stand before you to reclaim the urgent need for dialogue because deliberation and multilateral cooperation have been the operative platform upon which this organization was built and they remain the bridge to our future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I believe in a nation that looks in four directions at once.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: First, backwards.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: It remains true to the principles of the Charter.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I think the principles of the Charter are the moral and political compass of the international system.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Any reform must serve to better realise that founding vision.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Second, inward, to modernise and strengthen the institution.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: with transparency, efficiency and accountability, eliminating redundancies while eliminating field capacities, and turning reform from an episodic effort into continuous day-to-day commitment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Third, if I said backward, inward, third is outward.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: to reconnect with people.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The UN's legitimacy depends on its ability to deliver concrete results.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because if people do not see the UN working, they will not believe in multilateralism and forward to collectively design the future.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The Centennial of 2045 is a working horizon.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We must design the UN of the future today, with gender parity in the Secretariat, with real representation from all regions, and with the three pillars – peace and security, development and human rights – properly financed and balanced.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The United Nations was stressed on three deeply interconnected pillars, peace and security, sustainable development and human rights.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: On peace and security, prevention must lie at the heart of our action.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Quiet diplomacy, early warning, sustained engagement with real actors, and professional mediation are always more effective than reacting after a crisis already erupted.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The Secretary General must remain an active channel of communication even when consensus seems distant and must be present also in the field.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: On sustainable development, our priorities must be to eradicate hunger and poverty.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: reduce inequalities, close the digital divide, and advance meaningful climate action, among other things, so that we may leave young generations a world that offers them opportunity, dignity, and hope.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: This also requires a genuine reform of the international financial architecture, so that the most vulnerable countries are not left behind.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: On human rights, human dignity must remain at the center of everything the UN does.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Human rights are not only the foundation of a life lived in dignity, they are also a preventive instrument for last peace and global stability.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And central to this is the empowerment of women, girls and youth, whose full and meaningful participation in peace processes and decision-making is essential to building more just and resilient countries and societies.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I think that one of the unique elements of the United Nations is that it's the only irreplaceable space where humanity comes together to overcome common challenges in a world fragmented by disinformation, polarization, and competition of power.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The UN must be a place where difference becomes consensus, wars outweigh violence, and where collective decisions reflect a shared responsibility to preserve peace and human dignity.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The 21st century demands a UN
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: that anticipates, that prevents, that unites.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I am ready to lead that effort with experience, commitment and the deep conviction that multilateralism with the world changes lives.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Not surprisingly, you've emphasized how human rights has to be at the center of the leader of the United Nations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What makes you qualified?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And in fact, I should ask, why would you want this job?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: You saw when you were the High Commissioner, the tug between the world powers, between China and the United States, between speaking out in public and getting access in private.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What skill do you have that you can bring to the role?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, I think my skill is not only being at the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: It's the whole history of my life.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because my personal political trajectory has deeply changed myself.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Having been a young girl, when the Chicup d'etat happened in my country, my father, who really believed in rule of law, was put into prison and died by torture.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Me and my mom were also in prison and went to exile.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And then I learned what happens when institutions fail.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: when some people take power and don't respect the rule of law.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And then after we've been in exile, I learned the importance of multilateralism because I saw solidarity with the people who were suffering.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I think not only because of the help of countries, external countries, of course the battle for democracy in our own country, but so far for important countries, help Chile go back to democracy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: When I was elected president of Chile, the first woman president of my country, I also served twice, and also served as cabinet member, as health minister and defense minister, also the first in those positions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And as you know, as the first executive director of UN Women and afterwards as High Commissioner of Human Rights,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: In 20 years of my public life, both in national and international, I have combined the fundamental principles with realistic practical leadership.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But I have been in places where I have to deal with very complicated situations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And very powerful forces.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I have been able to do that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And also, another thing I think it gives me how the way I act is that I'm a physician, a pediatrician, and epidemiologist.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So I'm not only looking at the symptoms.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I'm looking at the root causes.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because if you don't understand what's there, you won't find the real solution.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And just one example.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Many countries have been there.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: every weekend fights, killings.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And it was not, there was different ethnic group, but it was not a matter of identity.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: It was a matter of lack of water and soil.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So if you want to find the solutions to those and prevent conflict happening, you need to understand really.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And for that, you need to be on the ground.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Okay.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, we've got a lot of questions for you, but first I have to ask you the one for eight billion campaign.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: You're, you're not, you know about it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: The UNA UK is obviously part of it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: They pulled in all candidates.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: to commit publicly to five principles, and everyone here is going to remember, so only tell the truth and nothing but the truth.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Publishing financial disclosures, engaging with civil society, participating in transparent General Assembly and Security Council processes, respecting Article 100 of the UN Charter, which means, of course, independence from member states, and avoiding conflicts of interest with any current UN role.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: They want those principles to define this campaign.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Do you commit to them, and why?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I have to add one.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: is only one mandate to be elected.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: That's also the one for 8 billion.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I could subscribe it now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I could describe it right now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: All of them.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: All of them, because that's what I've tried to do throughout my whole life.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: That's what I believe in.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I believe that you have to make the best decisions with independence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We have to appoint the best possible person.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I mean, there's always going to be pressures.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But I mean, in my life, I've shown that pressure doesn't
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I make decisions because I believe.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I was on the hearing at the General Assembly very happily, three hours.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: It was long, but it was really important because Member States could listen to what I was thinking about many issues.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I'm looking forward if there are hearings at the Security Council soon.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I've been trying to
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: have activities with civil society in this process.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So I think for the fifth principle that you know, for more than two years, I'm not part of the UN.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So I have no conflict of interest on that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Okay.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: We'll look forward to hearing you speaking out loudly if there's something happening behind closed doors.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Let's go to the audience.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: We have our first question from Captain Kibria, who's from the Dr. Mustafa Hazara Foundation, a non-profit
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: humanitarian foundation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Captain Kibriya, the floor is yours.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Oh, that's... God, if the captains didn't make it, then what about all the rest?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Okay, okay, we hope he wasn't prevented, and let me read his question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In today's increasingly complex global environment, do you believe the Secretary General should be entrusted with limited independent authority to undertake urgent peace and humanitarian initiatives when swift action is essential for the protection of human lives and international stability?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: So should you have the authority to intervene swiftly when lives are at stake?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: and needed to say when major powers are involved?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, you know, I think you can do a lot.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: You can do a lot as Secretary General.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: First of all, I mentioned the three key tools and
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: roles of the UN because I believe that when some countries say we need to go back to basics and think that peace and security is the only thing, I don't agree with them.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I think you won't have peace and security if you don't have development and you violate human rights.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So we need the three of them.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I will do my best to insist that the three of them needs to be supported, funded, et cetera.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The second thing is I think the charter has a lot of tools available, early warning, good offices, mediation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I am a person who's not waiting to do those things.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: If I realized that a conflict will come, I will try to go to anticipate myself to go before to the field.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And if it's not able to prevent it, to work on mediation, to use our good offices.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I mean, sometimes you try things and they don't work.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But I think you have to insist, Brian.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yes, because I think everyone here knows that all the major
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: wars of our time, be it Ukraine, be it Gaza, be it Sudan, be it the Iran war, the UN is not involved.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What can you do to change that?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What would you do differently?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, what I'll say, if there are many different reasons, because there has been sort of a tendency of sort of not supporting multilateral solution and supporting unilateral solution or mid-lab multilateral solutions.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I believe that there are always situations that group of countries can help, of course, that's fine, it's always been like that, or they can mediate, but it has to be under the coordination and coherence with the United Nations, with the Security Council.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But I do believe if I'm a Secretary General, I will go there to the field.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I won't wait until they invite me to go.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I will go there.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: If it's necessary, I'll be in front of the building of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because I believe the Secretary General is the, if I would say, the more important diplomat in the world.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And he has to be a moral voice.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So I will speak, but I will go to the field.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because there is discussion.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Should I be a secretary or a general?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: You'll be more general, given your defense.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I mean, you have to be the chief administrator, according to the charter.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But I have to be a general as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: OK.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I think that was a good answer.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Timoteo Olatunde is from the Timvert Empowerment Foundation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: It's a youth empowerment organization, mainly working in Nigeria.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: The question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Civil society organizations, particularly those led by young people in the Global South, face persistent barriers to meaningful UN participation, including accreditation delays, funding gaps, exclusion from decision-making,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What concrete steps will you take to ensure civil society is genuinely included in shaping UN policy?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Not merely consulted.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: In other words, just lip service.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Concrete.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, I remember when I was at UN Women, there was a group working with girls, and they said, I don't want to be at the table.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I want to decide the shape of the table.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I understand that's the same thing that these people are saying.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I believe, first of all, there's a lot that can be done with EcoSoc.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because ECOSOC is the one who accredits which are NGOs, civil society organizations that can be observers, that can participate.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So that can be, I would say, improved.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: All those procedures can be improved because it takes too long and that means that you have to ensure that more civil society organizations and NGOs can be present at the different places.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We need, though, I have to say, to ensure that they get visas.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: so they can participate in many of these because I saw in the CSW very few women from some parts of the world and I asked what was it?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: They said the cost of the ticket.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: No, they said they didn't get the visa.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So we need to ensure that that happens as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We need to enforce the Youth Office.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We need to support the Youth Office so it can make much more than what it's doing now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So I believe that also we have the solution 2250 that on youth and peace and security.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And I think we need to make it a reality because that is written, but you have to implement it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Another question.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Sivira Chaturvedi, formerly from UNIFEM, UNDP from the United States.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What actions will you take to address the current and growing anti-gender backlash by UN member states, including restrictions on civic spaces, which are threats to peace, development and especially women's human rights?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, everybody who knows me knows that I've been my whole life pushing for gender equality, for women's empowerment.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I'm continuing to do so as Secretary General or not as Secretary General.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So I think we need to continue
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: building upon what Secretary-General Guterres has done, because he has ensured that in all senior positions, women and men are parity, 50-50.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But that has to go down as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But also in the Secretariat, you have to have better representation, gender representation, geographical representation, et cetera.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We also need to continue speaking out loud about women's rights and to ensure that we don't have a bigger backlash.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I saw that backlash when I was new in women.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I will continue doing that job.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We need to build alliances with governments to push for gender rights.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And the other question, and the same with civic space.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We need people being part of the solution.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I mean, even in our common agenda, we said that we need to redefine what the international community was.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: It was not only about intergovernmental, it needed also to have all these other activists, civil society, academia, private sector, et cetera.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So that we need to continue opening these six spaces and trying also to reach out member states to continue doing so.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The clock is ticking, but I want to ask you, this is going to be a two-word answer, a one-word answer from you, because I want a very specific example.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: If you were the Secretary General, if you were going to deal with the, some would say, the apartheid policies of the Taliban in Afghanistan, one-word answer, would you engage or would you isolate them?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: I would engage.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Okay.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: And I did that as High Commissioner.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Okay.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: An audience question again, which I will also read.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Nicholas Candido, who's from Plataforma Osipo in Brazil, you may know it, Independent Women-led Policy Institute.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: When the UN was created, climate was not a primary concern.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Today, climate instability impacts livelihoods and ecosystems globally, yet the UN is ill-equipped to address its scale and urgency.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: What reforms would you implement to ensure adequate resources for an effective climate response?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: If I'm Secretary General, I have to maintain climate change in the agenda because some countries are still saying that climate change doesn't exist.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Some countries are discussing about going back to coal and other fossil combustibles and I think that's very dangerous.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So we need first of all to continue showing science with evidence why climate change is a reality and we need to maintain.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And then we need to think on how we finance it because climate action needs support.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And today, you know, the countries who have less contributed to the climate change, to the problems, are the ones who receive less money.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So we need to think of innovative mechanisms, for example, like debt climate swaps or other mechanisms that can ensure that countries who are not the responsible ones can really have the resources to do their mitigation and adaptation plans and so on.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Question from the floor, Freddy Carver.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Freddy Carver, good.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Captain Freddy Carver from ODI, the Independent Global Affairs Think Tank based in London.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Your question, Freddy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: One of the challenges for the UN-led humanitarian system is that it receives criticism from all sides.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: From crisis-affected populations who complain that their most important needs are often not met, particularly around safety and security.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: From actors in the global north, particularly on the right to politics, who argue that the system is fragmented, bureaucratic, inefficient, too expensive.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: and also from many voices in the global south who argue that the humanitarian system dominates space and funding apart from global south and civil society.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Yet the focus of the UN system in the last 12 months has been very much around cost cutting, around prioritization, and has struggled to meet some of these more strategic questions and challenges.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: How would you address those challenges if you became Secretary General?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Well, I think that one of the main tasks that the UN has to ensure is to protect lives and protect lives not only trying to avoid conflicts but also to deal with the results not only on conflict but because you need a military assistance also when you have diseases, when you have
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: natural disasters and so on.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So I believe that humanitarian assistance is key, but it has to be also related to the other areas that we need to work on because of course we have to provide humanitarian assistance when things are happening, but as I said before,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: we need to prevent them from happening because probably there will never be enough resources to solve all those problems.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And prevention is essentially to avoid not only the losses of life, the situation you mentioned, but also the cost that it means.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But on the other hand, I believe there is a huge group of people with a lot of experience that have done fantastic jobs on humanitarian assistance and I think we need to continue supporting them strongly.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But you worry that cost cutting would dominate your agenda, would have to dominate with almost the percentage of funding has gone down in recent years by nearly 25%.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Again, we're back to existential.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I know that the UNED reform is mainly being pushed because of cost cutting and because of lack of liquidity.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But if you have savings making all those initiatives that the reform intends to do, avoiding the redundancy and so on,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: That doesn't – that needs to be put, those money, in the areas that you prioritize.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Again, I will look like somebody who's repeating, but human rights, development, and peace and security.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because the thing of the reform is not to do more with less, it's to do better and put the money where the priorities are.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because you believe the critics who say it is too bloated, you do need to slim it down?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: No, I do believe that the UN has to be leaner,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: but muscle, not leaner but weak.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So one of the things that I believe and I will push strongly is that it can be a reform, it can be, it must be a reform, it's an opportunity as well, but what cannot happen is to diminish the field capacity.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And it cannot happen not to do what it needs to do because otherwise we're losing the essentials that make this new organization exist.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Michelle Bachelet, the world would want a UN Secretary General who's not just focused on the job, but also find ways to keep their mental health to keep sane.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: You have 30 seconds to give us a little hint.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: How does Michelle Bachelet relax?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Well, I'm not sure if I'm that master.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Because when things are very complicated, you need to be there energetically.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: The only thing that I want to say is that for me to be a secretary-general is not to be the glory of to be the first women secretary-general.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I have many glories in my life and I don't need more.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So what is your hobby?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: You've got five seconds.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: My hobby?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Read, dance.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Oh, dancing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Okay.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: But one thing I said, I would like to be a secretary-general if it's worthy.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: If I can be really independent, neutral, and serve all member states.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: That is what I would like.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, I think we would all want you to be able to achieve that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02

segments.text: Michelle Bachelet, thank you very much.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_02
